 {"id":"bd-1oyeg","title":"Parser: DA/DSR device queries + character set designation (G0/G1) + single shifts","description":"## Background
The VT parser handles 56 action variants covering most core terminal sequences, but lacks several important features needed for application compatibility.

## Gaps to Fill

### Device Attribute / Status Reports (Tier 1 — critical handshake)
- DA1 (CSI c / CSI 0 c): Primary device attributes — many CLI programs send this as a startup handshake
- DA2 (CSI > c / CSI > 0 c): Secondary device attributes (terminal type/version)
- DSR (CSI 5 n): Device status report — "are you OK?" query
- CPR (CSI 6 n): Cursor position report request

### Character Set Designation (Tier 1 — affects rendering)
- G0 designate (ESC ( B / ESC ( 0): ASCII / DEC Special Graphics
- G1 designate (ESC ) B / ESC ) 0): ASCII / DEC Special Graphics
- These are used by ncurses, vim, tmux for box-drawing characters

### Single Shift (Tier 2 — completeness)
- SS2 (ESC N): Single shift to G2
- SS3 (ESC O): Single shift to G3

- New Action variants for DA1, DA2, DSR, CPR, DesignateCharset, SingleShift
- Parser dispatch for all new sequences
- Unit tests for each new sequence
- Conformance fixtures for key sequences

 {"id":"bd-1oz","title":"Implement LRU width cache for text measurement","description":"# Implement LRU Width Cache for Text Measurement

## Background
Plan Section 1.4 and Appendix D.3 explicitly mention the "LRU width cache" from rich_rust as a key optimization. Text width measurement is a hot path (called for every render) and must be cached.

## Why LRU Cache?
- Unicode width calculation is expensive (grapheme segmentation + width lookup)
- Same text strings appear repeatedly (labels, status text, etc.)
- Cache hit rate expected to be high for typical UIs
- Prevents O(n) per-frame width recalculation

## Design
```rust
/// Width cache with LRU eviction
pub struct WidthCache {
    cache: LinkedHashMap<u64, usize>,  // hash -> width
    capacity: usize,
}

impl WidthCache {
    pub fn new(capacity: usize) -> Self {
        Self {
            cache: LinkedHashMap::with_capacity(capacity),
            capacity,
        }
    }

    /// Get cached width or compute and cache
    pub fn get_or_compute(&mut self, text: &str, compute: impl FnOnce() -> usize) -> usize {
        let hash = hash_text(text);
        
        if let Some(&width) = self.cache.get(&hash) {
            // Move to front (LRU refresh)
            self.cache.get_refresh(&hash);
            return width;
        }
        
        let width = compute();
        
        // Evict if over capacity
        while self.cache.len() >= self.capacity {
            self.cache.pop_front();
        }
        
        self.cache.insert(hash, width);
        width
    }
    
    pub fn clear(&mut self) {
        self.cache.clear();
    }
}

// Use FNV or xxhash for fast hashing
fn hash_text(text: &str) -> u64 {
    use std::hash::{Hash, Hasher};
    let mut hasher = FnvHasher::default();
    text.hash(&mut hasher);
    hasher.finish()
}
```

## Integration Points
- `RenderContext` holds a `RefCell<WidthCache>`
- `Text::cell_length()` uses the cache
- `Segment::cell_length()` uses the cache
- Cache is cleared on theme change (fonts can affect width)

## Cache Sizing
- Default: 1024 entries (covers typical UI with headroom)
- Configurable via `WidthCache::new(capacity)`
- Memory footprint: ~32 bytes per entry (hash + width + list pointers)

## Performance Requirements
- Cache hit: < 50ns (hash + lookup)
- Cache miss: < 5µs (grapheme segmentation + width calculation)
- Hash collision handling: rare, just recompute

## Tests Required
- [ ] Cache hits return correct cached value
- [ ] Cache misses compute and cache
- [ ] LRU eviction works correctly
- [ ] Clear() empties cache
- [ ] Thread safety if needed (RefCell for single-threaded)
- [ ] Performance benchmarks

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] LRU cache implemented with configurable capacity
- [ ] Integrated into text measurement hot path
- [ ] Performance benchmarks show significant speedup
- [ ] No correctness regressions in width measurement

 {"id":"bd-1plj","title":"Async Task Manager E2E: Anytime-Valid Flake Detector (e-process)","description":"## Goal
Detect flaky timing regressions in E2E tests without inflating false positives, using anytime-valid statistics.

## Core Idea (Alien Artifact)
Compute an e-value for latency/outcome deviations during E2E runs. For sub-Gaussian residuals r_t (mean 0 under null), use:
- e_t = exp(lambda * r_t - (lambda^2 * sigma^2) / 2)
- E_t = product_{i=1..t} e_i
Fail early only when E_t > 1/alpha, preserving validity under optional stopping.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Early fail only with strong evidence; no false alarms in stable runs.
- Works with variable-length test runs.
- Logs e-value trajectory and decision point.

## Tests (REQUIRED)
- unit_eprocess_threshold: failure at E_t > 1/alpha.
- unit_eprocess_nonnegative: e-values never negative.
- unit_optional_stopping: stopping early preserves decision validity (simulated).

## E2E (REQUIRED)
- Inject synthetic latency spikes; verify early failure and log evidence.
- No spike: no failure; log shows stable e-values.

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.

 {"id":"bd-1qb","title":"Implement Image widget with protocol detection","description":"PHASE 7 - EXTRAS: IMAGE WIDGET

## Background
Image widget displays images in terminal using Kitty Graphics Protocol, iTerm2 inline images, or Sixel fallback. Requires protocol detection and graceful degradation.

## Technical Requirements
- Protocol detection:
  - Kitty Graphics Protocol (XTGETTCAP query)
  - iTerm2 inline images (TERM_PROGRAM check)
  - Sixel (DA2 response parsing)
  - Fallback: ASCII/Braille representation
- Image loading via image crate
- Resize/fit modes: Contain, Cover, Stretch, None
- Aspect ratio preservation
- Partial update for animated GIFs
- Memory management for large images

## Design Rationale
Image support is a premium feature gated behind 'image' flag. Protocol detection happens once at startup and caches result. Fallback ensures graceful degradation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Protocol detection and caching
- [ ] Kitty Graphics Protocol implementation
- [ ] iTerm2 inline image implementation
- [ ] Sixel fallback (optional sub-feature)
- [ ] ASCII fallback when no protocol available
- [ ] Resize mode configuration
- [ ] Aspect ratio handling
- [ ] Feature gate: image

## Performance Budget
- Protocol detection: < 50ms (one-time)
- Image encode/transmit: proportional to size

 {"id":"bd-3e1t.6.1","title":"Spec: dirty-span model, invariants, and merge policy","description":"Background: dirty-row tracking is too coarse for sparse updates. Task: specify per-row span data model, merge/union policy, max span cap, overflow fallback to full-row scan, deterministic ordering, and reset semantics via clear_dirty. Considerations: scissor/opacity interactions, optional guard bands, config defaults, and evidence log fields. Output: written spec captured in notes.","notes":"Dirty-Span Model (Spec v1)

Motivation
- Dirty-row tracking is too coarse for sparse updates: scanning full rows wastes work.
- Dirty-span tracking records per-row modified x-ranges to reduce diff scan cost while preserving soundness.

Data Model
- For each row y, maintain a small ordered list of spans: Span { x0: u16, x1: u16 } half-open.
- Spans are non-overlapping, sorted by x0 ascending.
- Per-row full_row/overflow flag indicates the row should be scanned fully; when true, spans list is empty.
- Optional guard band (merge_gap) merges spans that are within ≤ merge_gap cells.

Invariants (Must Hold)
1) Soundness: for any cell (x,y) mutated since last clear, ∃ span in row y such that x ∈ [x0,x1).
2) Sorted + non-overlapping spans per row (after merge).
3) Deterministic merge order: given the same mutation sequence, span lists are identical.
4) Full-row flag dominates: if full_row[y] = true, spans[y] is empty and row is scanned fully.

Merge / Union Policy
- Insert span (x0,x1) into row y:
  - If full_row[y] => no-op (already full).
  - Insert at position by x0 (binary search recommended).
  - Merge with neighbors when overlaps or when gap ≤ merge_gap.
  - After merge, if span_count > max_spans_per_row => set full_row[y]=true and clear spans.
- Default implementation values (current): merge_gap = 1, max_spans_per_row = 64.
- Optional future: make these configurable; add coverage threshold to flip to full_row.

Mutation API (Conceptual)
- mark_dirty_span(y, x0, x1)
- mark_dirty_cell(y, x) => mark_dirty_span(y, x, x+1)
- mark_dirty_rect(rect): for each row in rect, call mark_dirty_span(y, rect.x, rect.right())

Reset Semantics
- clear_dirty_spans(): clears all spans and full_row flags.
- On Buffer::new, initialize all rows as full_row to guarantee first diff is full.

Interaction with Existing Dirty Rows
- Dirty-span is a strict refinement; dirty_rows is a coarse fallback.
- If both exist, diff strategy may choose:
  - full_row[y] => full scan (row-skip still allowed)
  - spans[y].is_empty() => skip row
  - spans[y] => scan only spans
- False positives are allowed; false negatives are forbidden.

Scissor / Opacity / Compositing

Determinism & Ordering
- Use Vec/SmallVec per row to avoid hash-based ordering.
- Merge order is deterministic: left-to-right; stable tie-breakers for equal x0.

- dirty_span_rows, dirty_span_total, dirty_span_overflows

Failure Modes + Fallbacks
- Excessive fragmentation => full_row fallback
- Span list overflow => full_row fallback
- Resize => re-init to full dirty

 {"id":"bd-3obms","title":"Improved Crossing Minimization (Sifting Algorithm)","description":"## Goal
Augment or replace the barycenter heuristic in diagram_layout.rs with the sifting algorithm for better edge crossing reduction. The existing barycenter approach is correct but produces suboptimal layouts for dense graphs (10+ nodes per layer).

## Background
- Current: barycenter_sort does bidirectional sweeps using neighbor position averages. O(n*E) per sweep, good results for sparse graphs.
- Sifting: for each node in a layer, try inserting it at every position and pick the one with minimum crossings. O(n^2 * E) per layer but finds significantly better solutions.
- The layout quality metrics already measure crossings — improvement is directly measurable.

## Implementation
1. Add sifting_sort() alongside barycenter_sort() in diagram_layout.rs.
2. For each layer, for each node: remove from position, count crossings at each alternate position, insert at best position.
3. Use the optimized O(1) crossing counter (the ref_member boolean array) from the barycenter fix.
4. Run sifting after barycenter (as a refinement pass) rather than as a replacement.
5. Budget-gated: sifting is more expensive; respect the computation budget.

## Expected Improvement
- 15-30% fewer crossings on graphs with 20+ nodes (based on graph drawing literature).
- No change for simple graphs (barycenter already optimal for small inputs).

## Files
- crates/ftui-extras/src/diagram_layout.rs (add sifting_sort, integrate with minimize_crossings)

 {"id":"bd-3r87","title":"Text Effects: Scanline / CRT Effects","description":"## Background
CRT/scanline effects create strong nostalgic retro appeal, perfect for terminal UIs. Simulates the look of old monitors applied to text.

## Implementation

Add TextEffect variant:
```rust
TextEffect::Scanline {
    intensity: f64,      // 0.0-1.0, how dark the scan lines (0.3 typical)
    line_gap: u8,        // Every Nth row dimmed (2 = every other row)
    scroll: bool,        // Lines scroll slowly for CRT feel
    scroll_speed: f64,   // Rows per second if scrolling
    flicker: f64,        // 0.0-1.0, random brightness variation
}
```

### Rendering:
```rust
fn apply_scanline(&self, color: PackedRgba, row: u16, time: f64) -> PackedRgba {
    // Determine if this row is a scanline
    let scroll_offset = if self.scroll { 
        (time * self.scroll_speed) as u16 
    } else { 
        0 
    };
    let is_scanline = (row + scroll_offset) % self.line_gap as u16 == 0;
    
    // Apply dimming
    let dim_factor = if is_scanline { 1.0 - self.intensity } else { 1.0 };
    
    // Apply flicker (random per-frame variation)
    let flicker_factor = 1.0 - self.flicker * random_for_frame(time);
    
    apply_alpha(color, dim_factor * flicker_factor)
}
```

### Note on Screen Effects:
Screen-wide effects like curvature, vignette, and barrel distortion are out of scope for text effects. Those would be terminal/screen-level post-processing, not text styling.

### Phosphor Glow:
For authentic CRT look, combine Scanline with a subtle Glow effect:
```rust
StyledText::new("RETRO")
    .effect(TextEffect::Scanline { intensity: 0.3, line_gap: 2, ... })
    .effect(TextEffect::Glow { color: green, intensity: 0.2, ... })
```

## Files
crates/ftui-extras/src/text_effects.rs

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)
- test_scanline_dims_every_nth: Correct rows affected
- test_scanline_intensity_applied: Color reduced by intensity
- test_scanline_scroll_moves_pattern: Pattern shifts with time
- test_scanline_flicker_varies: Different frames = different brightness
- test_scanline_gap_1_dims_all: Every row affected with gap=1
- test_scanline_gap_2_alternates: Every other row

## Visual Tests
- Snapshot multi-line text with scanlines, gap=2
- Snapshot same text at t=0 vs t=1 with scroll

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Text Effects: Scanline / CRT Effects
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-3sa7","title":"Demo Showcase: Snapshot/Time Travel Player","description":"## Background

FrankenTUI already embraces determinism (Buffer -> Diff -> Presenter). A natural superpower is "time travel":
- record frames / state snapshots
- replay deterministically
- inspect diffs and metadata

This screen turns the harness/snapshot infrastructure into a user-visible, jaw-dropping demo.

## UX / Core Interactions

- Timeline scrubber of recorded frames/snapshots.
- Playback controls: step back/forward, play/pause, jump to markers.
- Detail panel:
  - snapshot metadata (timestamp, terminal size, mode)
  - render stats (diff cell count, ANSI byte count)
  - optional: checksum/hash for integrity

Suggested keybindings:
- Space: play/pause
- Left/Right: step
- Home/End: first/last
- M: toggle markers

## Architecture Notes

- Use existing snapshot/time-travel capture mechanisms (ftui-harness / ftui-pty utilities).
- Store snapshots in a compact format:
  - buffer cells (or diffs)
  - metadata: size, mode, seed
- Optional integrity feature:
  - compute a hash chain over snapshots so replays are tamper-evident (useful for regression artifacts).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Can load a recorded snapshot set and scrub smoothly.
- Playback is deterministic under a fixed snapshot file.
- Displays at least one of:
  - per-frame diff cell count
  - per-frame ANSI byte count
  - checksum

Additionally (project-wide standards):
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

## Alien Artifact Upgrades (Concrete)

- "Isomorphism proof" view:
  - show that replay output matches original via checksums
  - surface the exact invariants being asserted

## Extreme Optimization

- Scrubbing must be responsive:
  - pre-index snapshots
  - avoid per-step allocations
  - enforce a budget for 10k-frame scrubs","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-02T22:31:29.210554198Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T18:09:09.085370464Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T18:09:09.085352471Z","close_reason":"Completed Snapshot/Time Travel Player screen implementation: timeline scrubbing, playback controls, frame metadata display (change count, checksum, memory size), markers, recording mode, diagnostic panel.
